Datapoints: AI Preparedness Index Score for Malaysia is 0.63

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No national AI strategy or policy; absent from government agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Draft strategy or formal consultations underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: National AI strategy published
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): AI strategy implemented with defined targets and allocated funding
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Comprehensive, regularly reviewed AI strategy with measurable outcomes and enforcement mechanisms
Datapoints:
- Malaysia's Artificial Intelligence Roadmap 2021-2025 (launched in Aug 2022 by Malaysian Ministry of Science, Technology, and Innovation (MOSTI))

- National AI Office est. in 2024 under the Ministry of Digital to  oversee implementation and coordination 

- As of the third quarter of 2024, Malaysia’s National AI Roadmap 2021-2025 has achieved 63% completion

Sub-Parameter: AI Governance and Regulatory Frameworks and Ethical AI Principles
Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No governance framework or regulatory discussion; No ethical AI discussion or principles
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Initial discussions on AI governance; no formal framework; Ethical AI principles under discussion or consultation
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: AI governance guidelines or regulatory approach published; Ethical AI principles published
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): AI governance framework and ethical AI principles operationalised
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: AI governance framework and ethical AI principles are legally binding; Regulatory body with oversight, enforcement, and review cycles
Datapoints:
- There is currently no specific legislation regulating the use of machine learning and AI in Malaysia. But the govt has put forward guidelines for the AI users, policy makers and developers, designers etc. 

- The intention of the National Guidelines on AI Governance & Ethics (AIGE) is to develop and deploy AI in a safe, trustworthy, and ethical manner, taking an approach to developing and deploying AI from both an ethical and legal point of view

- The guidelines are not legally binding and are for the voluntary adoption of the seven AI Principles by developers and deployers of AI alongside existing laws to ensure that the adoption of AI technology

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No data protection legislation
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Draft data protection law or consultation underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Data protection legislation enacted
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Data protection law enforced with designated authority and complaint mechanisms
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Data protection law enforced with strong redressal mechanism
Datapoints:
- Malaysia's Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024

- PDPA Act's enforcement is overseen by the Department of Personal Data Protection (JPDP)

-As per the UNDP Digital Development Compass, Malaysia has a score of 4.17 in data and privacy and  5 in cybersecurity. (5 is the top score representing transformational performance). It has dynamic Legal Framework: Comprehensive law, baselines, compliance, international alignment.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No government AI initiatives or pilots
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Ad hoc pilots initiated by individual agencies
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: AI projects implemented in some public-sector domains with defined scope
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multiple AI deployments across ministries and public services; government AI programs with funding and targets
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Large-scale AI deployment across government; national AI program with R&D, compute, and talent investment
Datapoints:
- The National AI Roadmap has defined 11 national AI Use cases across 5 sectors (Agriculture and forestry, healthcare, smart cities and transportation, education and public services)

-Both Federal Government and the State Governments have embarked on numerous AI initiatives.
- Examples:
a. Healthcare:  Ministry of Health unveiled “DR. MATA”, an AI-powered diagnostic tool to detect diabetic retinopathy, a leading cause of blindness among diabetics.

AI is also digitally transforming the operations of public health clinics, with the roll-out of cloud-based digital management system (CCMS), enhancing efficiency and accessibility of healthcare services. 

b. Smart cities and transportation: AI adoption is traffic management. In Kuala Lumpur, a citywide CCTV network enables AI platforms to count vehicles, classify them by type, and recognise licence plates. This allows real-time traffic analysis

c.Financial services: In August 2024, the Central Bank of Malaysia, in partnership with PayNet, and other financial institutions, launched the National Fraud Portal (NFP), utilising AI to combat financial fraud through predictive analysis. 

d. Agriculture: Malaysia launched the world’s first AI-driven smart palm oil mill
Remarks, if any:
Multiple use cases have been outlined by the Govt

Objectives for each Use case have been specified,  specific budgets and measured KPIs not publically disclosed

Though specific budgets are not available, qualitatively this ecosystem tends towards mature ecosystem

Datapoints:
- Malaysia Digital Economy Blueprint 2021-30 I RM70 bn investment in digitalisation

-MyDIGITAL is the Government of Malaysia's national strategy to transform the country into a digitally-enabled, technology-driven high-income nation by 2030. It is organised around six strategic thrusts, 22 strategies, 48 national initiatives, and 28 sectoral initiatives, implemented across three phases.
Remarks, if any: Progress reports for Digital Intereventions avaiable on their webiste

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No designated lead agency for AI/DPI; no formal initiative; no cross-government coordination
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Single ministry or agency leading digital transformation; no cross-government coordination mechanism
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Inter-ministerial coordination mechanism established
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Inter-ministerial coordination mechanism established; lead agency with clear mandate
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Clear roles across national and sector agencies; continuous, transparent coordination and regular review meetings
Datapoints:
To support the implementation of digital functions, two departments and six agencies are placed under the Ministry of Digital.

1. National Digital Department (JDN), 
2. Department of Personal Data Protection (JPDP),
3. Malaysian Digital Economy Corporation (MDEC)
4. MyDIGITAL Corporation
5. CyberSecurity Malaysia (CSM)
6. Digital Nasional Berhad (DNB) 
7. MYNIC Berhad . The government has
8. National AI Office (NAIO) -implement and coordinate all matters related to AI governance and ethics

- These agencies coordinate with other ministries and departments

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No private sector engagement in digital agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Ad hoc engagement with select tech or telecom operators
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Participation or consultation with private sector; PPP frameworks emerging
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Formal public-private platforms operational; DPG and open-source ecosystem engaged; fintech and cloud providers integrated in delivery
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Deeper public-private integration; private sector co-invests in national digital infrastructure
Datapoints:
Existing partnerships between NAIO and other companies - 

1. Toshiba
2. Global AI Village
3. YTL POwer International Bhd
4. Microsoft
5. Google
6. Amazon Web Services

Malaysia is home to 284 AI technology companies, aiming to grow to
900 by 2026

Details:
Existing partnerships between NAIO and other companies - 

1. Toshiba - Partnering with MADA to integrate TOSHIBA’s Weather Forecast AI technology into the Smart Farming Project (Projek Ladang Padi IR4.0) in Kedah's Muda region

2. Global AI Village - Partnering with NAIO to incubate high-impact AI applications for Malaysian corporations and government in energy, cybersecurity, agriculture, supply chain, transportation, and healthcare

3. YTL Power International Bhd - Developing Ilmu 0.1, a Malaysian LLM built, owned, and operated by Malaysians in Malaysia.

4. Microsoft - Partnering with NAIO to launch a national AI Adoption Accelerator, promoting AI in key sectors like the public sector and creating an AI Governance Sandbox framework.

5. Google - Running a pilot project with the Ministry of Digital to equip 100 officers with AI-powered solutions to automate tasks, analyze data, and streamline workflows, enabling focus on higher-value work and citizen engagement.

6. Amazon Web Services - Partnering with NAIO to empower Malaysians with AI skills and infrastructure for national and global ecosystem development.

The private sector integrates AI across various industries including medical and healthcare, agriculture, transportation, education, business and finance. Companies such as Petronas, JCorp, WISE AI, and Phison are leading adopters. Industry enablers such as YTL and Iskandar Investment actively champion smart city initiatives and AI ecosystem development.
Remarks, if any: Private sector is engaging with the Govt to run capacity building programs, increase compute capacity

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No MDB or bilateral engagement
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: MDB/donor activity exploratory; primarily providing advisory or strategy support
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Recurrent MDB and bilateral investment in AI/DPI projects; approved and pipeline financing visible
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multi-year strategic partnerships with ADB, World Bank, bilaterals operational
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Development partners fully aligned to national roadmap
Datapoints: The Malaysia Centre for Fourth Industrial Revolution (MYCentre4IR) was established in 2023 and is a partnership between the Malaysian government and the World Economic Forum (WEF). It brings together policymakers, business leaders, and experts to develop policies for advancing frontier technologies in Malaysia. As a strategic partner to NAIO, MYCentre4IR will connect NAIO with global WEF experts, support international partnerships, and co-develop thought leadership publications.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No domestic academic or research engagement in AI/DPI
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Isolated university or think tank activity; no formal linkage to government agenda
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: A few institutions(2-3) formally engaged in AI/DPI research; outputs informing policy discussions
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multiple universities and think tanks contributing to AI/DPI policy
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: National research ecosystem in the country- shaping national and regional policy
Datapoints:
Key research centres addressing AI and ethics in Malaysia include:
1. The Centre for Artificial Intelligence and Robotics (CAIRO) at Universiti Teknologi Malaysia (UTM)
2. The Artificial Intelligence Research Unit (AiRU) at Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS)
3. Malaya Artificial Intelligence Research (MALAYA AIR) at Universiti Malaya (UM)
4.  The Artificial Intelligence and Applications Research Team at the University of Nottingham Malaysia
5. The Artificial Intelligence Research Group at Universiti Malaysia Sarawak (UNIMAS)

Academic institutions like UTM and UM contribute through R&D, curriculum development, and talent pipelines via centres of excellence like the Centre of Artificial Intelligence and Robotics (CAIRO), UTM.

Associations and civil society groups, including PIKOM, MIFA, and Women in AI, promote AI literacy, digital inclusion, and gender equity. Their efforts ensure women and underserved groups benefit from AI, fostering trust and cultural sensitivity in adoption.

Datapoints:
Malaysia has attracted substantial private investments in AI technologies, underscoring the
sector’s potential. Notable investments include:
-Microsoft: USD 2.2 billion for cloud and AI services.
-Google: USD 2 billion for its first data centre and Google Cloud region.
-ByteDance: Approximately USD 2.13 billion for an AI hub.
-Oracle: Over USD 6.5 billion to establish its first public cloud region.
